1:
On Numbers 2:1 — “And the LORD spake unto Moses and unto Aaron, saying” — the witnesses mark the divine ordering of the camp.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: Moses is commanded to teach Israel how to pitch their tents and set up the ensigns of their fathers’ houses. The Puritan Henry: a general appointment for orderly encampment when they rested and orderly march when they moved. The Reformed Gill: spoken after the numbering, as the camp was about to set forward. Calvin: this distribution into bands served to prevent contention, for ambition is natural to man.

The verse opens the ordering. God speaks to Moses and Aaron to arrange the camp. The host is to be set in order by his command.

So the witnesses see God himself take in hand the ordering of the great host, that all might be done by his appointment. The witnesses note the timing and occasion Gill observes: the word comes after the numbering of the people (ch. 1), as the congregation with the tabernacle was about to set forward on its march; the army, now counted, must be arranged. They mark Henry’s summary that this is a general appointment governing both their encampment when at rest and their order of march when they moved; some order they had kept since coming out of Egypt, but now it is fixed by divine law. They observe Clarke’s note that Moses was commanded to teach the people how to pitch their tents and to erect the ensigns of their fathers’ houses, so that each knew his place. They observe the deeper purpose Calvin draws out: this distribution into separate bands served to prevent strife, for so natural is ambition to man that, had not God assigned to each their proper position, they would have quarrelled for the place of honour; the divine arrangement forestalled envy. They note that God is a God of order, not of confusion, ordering even the tents of a wandering people. They observe that the whole camp was framed around the tabernacle, God’s dwelling at the centre. The verse thus opens the ordering: God commands the arrangement of the camp — the manner of it given in the next verse.
2:
On Numbers 2:2 — “Every man of the children of Israel shall pitch by his own standard, with the ensign of their father’s house: far off about the tabernacle of the congregation shall they pitch” — the witnesses mark the standards and the reverent distance.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the ensigns. Keil and Delitzsch: each by his standard, with the ensign of his father’s house, far off about the tabernacle. Adam Clarke: much pains have been taken to illustrate the best method of encamping such a host. The Reformed Gill: four standards or banners, three tribes under each. Wesley: four great standards, distinguished by colours or figures; lesser ensigns for each family; “far off,” in reverence.

The verse sets the standards. Each pitches by his standard and family ensign, at a reverent distance round the tabernacle. The camp is ordered about God’s dwelling.

So the witnesses see the host arranged under standards, set in order and reverence about the tabernacle at the centre. The witnesses note the banners: “every man… shall pitch by his own standard, with the ensign of their father’s house” — Gill and Wesley explain that there were four great standards or banners, each marking one of the four camps of three tribes, distinguished (the witnesses suppose) by their colours or figures; and that besides these, each father’s house or family had its own lesser ensign, so that every man knew both his tribe’s standard and his family’s sign, and could find his place. They mark the labour of commentators Clarke notes, who have taken great pains to work out the best method of encamping so vast a multitude in order. They observe the reverent distance: “far off about the tabernacle” — Wesley draws out that the tribes were to pitch at a respectful distance from the sanctuary, neither crowding upon it nor leaving it; the space between marked their reverence for the holy dwelling, and (the Levites encamping nearest) guarded it from profane approach. They note the pattern: the tabernacle in the midst, the Levites immediately around it, and the twelve tribes in four camps at a distance beyond — God’s dwelling the heart of the whole, all ordered about his presence. They observe the typology the witnesses suggest: so the church is ordered about God in its midst, each in his place, near yet reverent. They note that order and reverence go together. The verse thus sets the standards: each by his banner, far off about the tabernacle — the east camp named first in the next verse.
3:
On Numbers 2:3 — “And on the east side toward the rising of the sun shall they of the standard of the camp of Judah pitch… Nahshon the son of Amminadab shall be captain of the children of Judah” — the witnesses mark Judah set first, on the east.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the place of honour. The Puritan Henry: God appointed their place, to prevent strife and envy; Judah led the first squadron. The Reformed Gill: the east, the place “before.” Wesley: Judah in the first post, leading the van, both as most numerous and chiefly because Christ, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, was to descend from it, from the loins of Nahshon.

The verse sets Judah first. On the east, toward the sunrise, Judah leads under his standard. The royal and messianic tribe takes the foremost place.

So the witnesses see Judah given the foremost place, on the east toward the rising sun, leading the whole host. The witnesses note the position of honour: “on the east side… the camp of Judah” — the east, the quarter of the sunrise and (as Gill notes) the place reckoned “before,” was the front of the camp and the head of the march; there Judah pitched and from there led the van. They mark Henry’s point that God himself appointed each tribe its place, to prevent strife and envy; the order was not of man’s contriving but of God’s, so that none could complain. They observe the reasons the witnesses give for Judah’s primacy: Wesley notes Judah was the most numerous tribe, and so fitly led; but chiefly that the place of honour was given it because Christ, the Lion of the tribe of Judah (Genesis 49:9), was to descend from it — indeed from the loins of this very Nahshon, named here as captain, who stands in the genealogy of the Messiah (Matthew 1:4). They observe the captain appointed: Nahshon the son of Amminadab, prince of Judah, an ancestor of David and of Christ. They note the typology the witnesses press: that the tribe from which the King and Saviour would come was set at the head of God’s people, leading them, as Christ leads his church; the Lion of Judah goes before. They observe that the order of the camp honoured the line of promise. The verse thus sets Judah first: on the east, leading the host — his number given in the next verse.
4:
On Numbers 2:4 — “And his host, and those that were numbered of them, were threescore and fourteen thousand and six hundred” — the witnesses mark the muster of Judah.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: “his host, and those that were numbered of them” — the army according to its numbering, as repeated for each tribe (Numbers 2:6). The Reformed Gill: the number as before, in the first chapter.

The verse numbers Judah’s host. They were 74,600. The leading tribe is the most numerous.

So the witnesses see the muster of Judah set down, the largest of all the tribes. The witnesses note the formula that recurs through the chapter: “his host, and those that were numbered of them” — Keil and Delitzsch explain this as the army of the tribe according to its numbering, the same phrase repeated for each tribe in turn (Numbers 2:6 and following); each tribe is reckoned as a host, an army, under its captain. They mark the figure: seventy-four thousand six hundred — the same count, Gill observes, that was taken in the numbering of the first chapter; the arrangement of the camp builds directly on the census just made. They observe that Judah’s host was the greatest in number, fitting the tribe set at the head of the march; the leading place was matched by the leading strength. They note that the people are reckoned not as a disorganized crowd but as marshalled armies, each tribe a regiment with its tale of fighting men; Israel goes forth as the host of the LORD. They observe that the numbering, here applied to the camp, served both order and war: the men of war counted, arrayed under standards, ready to march and to fight. They note the providence that the royal tribe, the line of Christ, was also the most numerous and strong. They observe that God’s ordering joined honour and strength in Judah. The verse thus numbers Judah’s host: 74,600 — the tribes pitched next to him following in the next verses.

So the witnesses, beyond the bare number, see in this muster a token of the whole ordered host. They observe that the reckoning of Judah as a host, an army, frames the camp not as a wandering crowd but as the disciplined array of the LORD, marshalled tribe by tribe under their captains. They note that the leading place and leading strength of Judah went together by God’s appointment, the royal and messianic tribe set both first and greatest, that the line of promise might go before the people. They mark that the same exact form — “his host, and those that were numbered of them” — recurs for every tribe in turn, so that none is reckoned more carefully than another; the great God who set the stars in order numbers and arrays the tents of his people, that all may move and rest at his word.
5:
On Numbers 2:5 — “And those that do pitch next unto him shall be the tribe of Issachar: and Nethaneel the son of Zuar shall be captain of the children of Issachar” — the witnesses mark Issachar set with Judah.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: those that pitch next, the tribe of Issachar, with Nethaneel its captain. The Reformed Gill: Issachar, with Zebulun, brethren of Judah by the same mother Leah, fitly put together.

The verse joins Issachar to Judah. Issachar pitches next, under the same standard. The brothers by Leah are grouped together.

So the witnesses see Issachar placed beside Judah in the eastern camp, under the same leading standard. The witnesses note the placement: “those that do pitch next unto him shall be the tribe of Issachar” — Issachar took his station next to Judah, one of the three tribes gathered under Judah’s banner on the east. They mark the fitness Gill observes in the grouping: Issachar and Zebulun, the two tribes set with Judah, were his full brothers, the sons of the same mother Leah; it was fitting that those born of one mother should encamp together, the natural bond of kinship strengthening the order of the camp. They observe the captain named: Nethaneel the son of Zuar, prince of Issachar, appointed (as each tribe’s prince) to lead his host and to have charge of its ordering. They note that God’s arrangement was not arbitrary but wise, joining kindred tribes that brotherly affection might knit the camp and lessen occasion of strife; the families of Leah kept together as one company. They observe that even in the marshalling of an army, God regarded the ties of blood and affection. They note the dignity given to each tribe in having its own prince and place, none neglected, all ordered. They observe that the camp was a great family ordered about its God. The verse thus joins Issachar to Judah: next under the standard — his number given in the next verse.

So the witnesses see in this placing of Issachar a pattern that runs through the whole camp: that God grouped the tribes by the ties of their mothers, the sons of Leah set together on the east, that the bonds of kinship might knit each company and lessen all occasion of strife. They note that every tribe, set under its leading standard, was yet given its own prince and host, none merged or lost, each owned in its place. They mark that the order was not of man’s contriving but of God’s, who assigned to each their station that ambition might find no room. They observe that so the church is to be ordered, each in his own calling and rank about the God in its midst, the members knit in love and kept from envy by the wise appointment of their Head, that the whole body may move as one.
6:
On Numbers 2:6 — “And his host, and those that were numbered thereof, were fifty and four thousand and four hundred” — the witnesses mark the muster of Issachar.

The Reformed Gill: the host of Issachar, numbered as before in the first chapter — fifty-four thousand four hundred.

The verse numbers Issachar. They were 54,400. The second tribe of the eastern camp is counted.

So the witnesses see the host of Issachar reckoned, the second of the three tribes under Judah’s standard. The witnesses note the figure Gill records: fifty-four thousand four hundred fighting men, the same number taken in the census of the first chapter; the camp arrangement rests, as throughout, on the numbering already made. They mark the recurring pattern of the chapter, each tribe named, its captain appointed, and its host counted: the enumeration is deliberate and complete, that every tribe’s strength be known and its place fixed. They observe that Issachar, though set under Judah’s lead, was a great host in its own right, more than fifty thousand strong; each tribe brought its own army to the muster of the LORD. They note that the careful counting of each tribe, however repetitive, testifies to God’s particular regard for his people: he numbers them tribe by tribe, as a shepherd numbers his flock, none overlooked. They observe that the ordered camp was no abstraction but a real array of hundreds of thousands of men, each in his appointed place. They note that the strength of the eastern camp was gathering, Judah and Issachar together already near a hundred and thirty thousand. They observe that God’s host was mighty and well ordered. The verse thus numbers Issachar: 54,400 — the third tribe, Zebulun, following in the next verse.

So the witnesses see, in the counting of Issachar, the steady witness of the whole chapter: that God numbers his people one by one, tribe by tribe, none too small or too obscure to be reckoned and given a place. They observe that the careful, repeated enumeration, plain as it is, declares God’s particular care for every part of his host; he tells them as a shepherd tells his flock. They note that Issachar, though set under Judah’s lead, brought its own army of many thousands to the muster, a full and honoured member of the host. They mark that the strength of the eastern camp was gathering tribe by tribe, the brother-sons of Leah making one mighty company under the standard of the tribe of promise. They observe that the ordered host, so numbered, was a thing of beauty and strength, of which it could be said, “How goodly are thy tents, O Jacob” (Numbers 24:5).
7:
On Numbers 2:7 — “Then the tribe of Zebulun: and Eliab the son of Helon shall be captain of the children of Zebulun” — the witnesses mark Zebulun, third in the eastern camp.

The Reformed Gill: Zebulun pitched next; yet the order was not simply Judah, Issachar, Zebulun in a row, for Judah lay between the two, the one before him and the other behind.

The verse adds Zebulun. The third tribe of the east is set under Judah’s standard. The eastern camp is completed.

So the witnesses see Zebulun added as the third tribe of the eastern camp, completing Judah’s company. The witnesses note the placement Gill carefully describes: Zebulun pitched next, yet the order of the three tribes was not simply Judah, then Issachar, then Zebulun ranged in a single line; rather Judah, the leader, lay in the midst of his standard, with Issachar before him on one side and Zebulun behind on the other, the banner in the centre of the three. They mark that Zebulun, like Issachar, was a son of Leah, full brother to Judah; so the eastern camp was wholly composed of Leah’s sons (Judah, Issachar, Zebulun), the children of one mother kept together under one standard. They observe the captain named: Eliab the son of Helon, prince of Zebulun, set over his host as the other princes over theirs. They note the orderliness the witnesses keep drawing out: every tribe had its fixed place and its appointed leader, so that the camp, though vast, was arranged with the precision of a marshalled army, each man knowing where to pitch and whom to follow. They observe that the grouping by mother bound the eastern camp in a natural unity, the three brother-tribes encamped as one. They note that God’s wisdom appears in so ordering the host that affection and order met. They observe that the leading camp was now complete in its three tribes. The verse thus adds Zebulun: third under Judah’s standard — his number given in the next verse.

So the witnesses see in Zebulun’s placing the same wise care that ordered all the camp: the leader set in the midst of his standard, the brother-tribes ranged before and behind, the whole company of Leah’s sons knit as one about the banner of Judah. They observe that every tribe had both its fixed station and its appointed captain, so that the vast host, though hundreds of thousands strong, was arranged with the precision of a marshalled army, each man knowing where to pitch and whom to follow. They note that the grouping by mother bound the eastern camp in a natural unity, affection strengthening order. They mark that God, who is not the author of confusion but of peace, so framed the tents of his people that none need quarrel for place. They observe that the church, likewise ordered by her Head, keeps her ranks in love, each serving in his own station about the God who dwells in her midst.
8:
On Numbers 2:8 — “And his host, and those that were numbered thereof, were fifty and seven thousand and four hundred” — the witnesses mark the muster of Zebulun.

The Reformed Gill: the host of Zebulun, numbered as before — fifty-seven thousand four hundred.

The verse numbers Zebulun. They were 57,400. The third tribe of the east is counted.

So the witnesses see the host of Zebulun reckoned, completing the count of the eastern camp. The witnesses note the figure Gill gives: fifty-seven thousand four hundred fighting men, the same number as in the first chapter’s census; the camp’s arrangement, as always, builds on the numbering already taken. They mark that Zebulun, the third of Judah’s company, was itself a strong host of more than fifty-seven thousand, second in the eastern camp only to Judah; the brother-tribes together made a mighty array. They observe the steady, deliberate manner of the record: tribe by tribe, captain and number, the same form repeated, that nothing be left uncertain in the ordering of the host; God would have his people arrayed with exactness. They note that the careful enumeration, however plain and repetitive, bears its own witness: every tribe is owned, counted, and given its place, none too small to be reckoned, none left out of the order. They observe that the eastern camp, now complete, was the greatest of the four, fitting the tribe of Judah at its head and the line of Christ that led it. They note that the strength and order of the host reflected the God who arrayed it. They observe that the people went forth not in confusion but as the marshalled army of the LORD. The verse thus numbers Zebulun: 57,400 — the total of Judah’s camp gathered in the next verse.

So the witnesses see, in the muster of Zebulun, the completing of the eastern camp’s strength, the three brother-tribes of Leah making by far the greatest of the four companies. They observe that the steady recording — tribe, captain, and number, the same form unvaried — bears witness that God would have his host arrayed with exactness, every tribe owned and counted, none left uncertain in the ordering. They note that the leading camp, fitted with the most men, was set under the tribe of Judah, from which Christ the Lion would come, that the strength of the host might be in its van. They mark that the careful counting, however plain, declares God’s regard for each part of his people, the small reckoned no less than the great. They observe that the people, so numbered and arrayed, went forth not in confusion but as the disciplined army of the LORD, ordered about his dwelling.
9:
On Numbers 2:9 — “All that were numbered in the camp of Judah were an hundred thousand and fourscore thousand and six thousand and four hundred… These shall first set forth” — the witnesses mark the total of Judah’s camp and its leading the march.

The Reformed Gill: all numbered in the camp of Judah — one hundred eighty-six thousand four hundred; these, divided into their armies, set forth first.

The verse totals Judah’s camp. The three tribes numbered 186,400, and led the march. The greatest camp goes first.

So the witnesses see the eastern camp summed and set in the front of the march. The witnesses note the total Gill records: all that were numbered in the camp of Judah — Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun together — were a hundred and eighty-six thousand four hundred men; the three brother-tribes of Leah made by far the largest of the four camps. They mark the order of march: “these shall first set forth” — the camp of Judah, when the host moved, led the way, going before the tabernacle and the other tribes; the foremost place in the camp was also the foremost in the march. They observe the fitness the witnesses have drawn out: that the tribe of Judah, from which Christ the Lion would come, both encamped at the head on the east and led the whole host forward; the line of promise went before God’s people. They note that the greatest in number was set to lead, that the strength of the host be in its van. They observe the typology: as Judah led Israel, so Christ, the true Judah, leads his church through the wilderness of this world, going before them. They note that the ordered march, like the ordered camp, was wholly of God’s appointing, each camp knowing when to move. They observe that the host went forth in due order, the leading tribe first. The verse thus totals Judah’s camp and sets it first: 186,400, leading the march — the southern camp of Reuben following in the next verses.

So the witnesses see in the leading of Judah’s camp a figure of Christ going before his people. They observe that the greatest in number was set both at the head of the camp and at the front of the march, the tribe of promise leading Israel through the wilderness as the true Judah, the Lion of the tribe, leads his church through the wilderness of this world. They note that the order of the march, like the order of the camp, was wholly of God’s appointing, each company knowing when to move and in what rank, that the great multitude might go forward without confusion. They mark that the foremost place, both in pitching and in marching, was given to the line from which the Saviour would spring, so that the honour of the camp followed the promise. They observe that the host went forth in due order, the leading tribe first, the whole array moving as one at the word of the LORD.
10:
On Numbers 2:10 — “On the south side shall be the standard of the camp of Reuben according to their armies: and the captain of the children of Reuben shall be Elizur the son of Shedeur” — the witnesses mark Reuben’s camp on the south.

The Reformed Gill: the camp of Reuben, with Simeon and Gad, on the south, divided into lesser bodies or companies; Elizur the son of Shedeur its captain.

The verse sets Reuben on the south. The second camp, of Reuben, Simeon, and Gad, pitches there. The southern quarter is assigned.

So the witnesses see the second camp, led by Reuben, placed on the south side of the tabernacle. The witnesses note the position: “on the south side shall be the standard of the camp of Reuben” — Gill observes that this camp consisted of the tribes of Reuben, Simeon, and Gad, gathered under Reuben’s banner and ranged on the southern quarter; the camp was divided into its lesser bodies or companies, here called armies. They mark the captain named: Elizur the son of Shedeur, prince of Reuben, set over the host as the princes over the others. They observe the place given to Reuben: though Reuben was the firstborn of Jacob, and might have claimed the foremost place, that honour had passed from him (for his sin, Genesis 49:4) to Judah; yet Reuben is not cast off, but given the leading of the second camp, the next in dignity after Judah’s. They note the providence the witnesses see: God’s ordering both honoured Judah with the first place and yet left the firstborn Reuben a place of leadership, that none be without honour, while the line of promise held the chief room. They observe that the south camp was thus set in its order, as the east had been, each in the quarter God appointed. They note that the tabernacle, with the east camp before it, now had the south camp on its right hand. They observe that the whole was rising into ordered form about God’s dwelling. The verse thus sets Reuben on the south: the second camp assigned — Simeon pitched by him in the next verses.
11:
On Numbers 2:11 — “And his host, and those that were numbered thereof, were forty and six thousand and five hundred” — the witnesses mark the muster of Reuben.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: his host and those numbered, forty-six thousand five hundred, as for each tribe. The Reformed Gill: the host of Reuben, distinct from his camp, of which it was but a part.

The verse numbers Reuben. They were 46,500. The leading tribe of the south is counted.

So the witnesses see the host of Reuben reckoned, the head of the southern camp. The witnesses note the figure: forty-six thousand five hundred, the same number, Gill observes, as in the census of the first chapter; the arrangement rests on the numbering already taken. They mark Gill’s careful distinction between “host” and “camp”: the host of Reuben was the tribe’s own army of forty-six thousand, distinct from the “camp” of Reuben, which was the whole company of three tribes (Reuben, Simeon, Gad) gathered under his standard; each tribe a host, the three together a camp. They observe that Reuben, though leading the second camp, was smaller in number than Judah; the firstborn’s host did not match the royal tribe’s, the strength as well as the honour having largely passed to Judah. They note the steady recording, tribe by tribe, that marks the whole chapter: name, captain, and number, the order kept exact. They observe that each tribe, great or small, was owned and counted in its place; God reckoned the lesser hosts as truly as the greater. They note that Reuben, set over his camp, brought a substantial army of his own to the muster. They observe that the southern camp was beginning to be told. The verse thus numbers Reuben: 46,500 — Simeon, who pitched by him, following in the next verse.

So the witnesses see, in the muster of Reuben, that the firstborn, though the foremost place had passed from him to Judah, was yet given the leading of the second camp and a substantial host of his own. They observe the care of the record to distinguish his “host” — the tribe’s own army — from his “camp,” the whole company of three tribes under his standard; each tribe a host, the three together a camp. They note that God’s ordering both honoured Judah with the first place and yet left Reuben a place of dignity, that none be without honour while the line of promise held the chief room. They mark that the strength as well as the precedence had largely passed to Judah, Reuben’s host falling short of the royal tribe’s. They observe that each tribe, great or small, was owned and reckoned in its station, the God of order numbering all his people and giving to each his place about the tabernacle.
12:
On Numbers 2:12 — “And those which pitch by him shall be the tribe of Simeon: and the captain of the children of Simeon shall be Shelumiel the son of Zurishaddai” — the witnesses mark Simeon set with Reuben.

The Reformed Gill: Simeon pitched under Reuben’s standard, on one side of it; Reuben and Simeon being brothers by the mother as well as the father, might well agree together.

The verse joins Simeon to Reuben. Simeon pitches by him under the same standard. The brothers by Leah are grouped on the south.

So the witnesses see Simeon placed beside Reuben in the southern camp, under the same banner. The witnesses note the placement: “those which pitch by him shall be the tribe of Simeon” — Simeon took his station next to Reuben, one of the three tribes under his standard on the south. They mark the fitness Gill observes in the grouping: Reuben and Simeon were brothers by the same mother as well as the same father, both sons of Leah; so, as Judah’s camp gathered Leah’s sons on the east, the southern camp too joined brothers of one mother, that natural affection might bind them and lessen occasion of strife. They observe the captain named: Shelumiel the son of Zurishaddai, prince of Simeon, set over his host. They note the consistent principle of the arrangement that the witnesses have traced: kindred tribes encamped together, the children of one mother under one standard, so that the camp was knit by the bonds of blood as well as ordered by divine command. They observe that Simeon, whose place at his father’s blessing had been clouded (with Levi, for their violence, Genesis 49:5), is yet given his tribe, captain, and place in the host, not cast off but ordered with the rest. They note that God’s arrangement was both wise and gracious, finding a place for every tribe. They observe that the southern camp, like the eastern, was bound by kinship. The verse thus joins Simeon to Reuben: by him under the standard — his number given in the next verse.
13:
On Numbers 2:13 — “And his host, and those that were numbered of them, were fifty and nine thousand and three hundred” — the witnesses mark the muster of Simeon.

The Reformed Gill: the host of Simeon, numbered as before — fifty-nine thousand three hundred.

The verse numbers Simeon. They were 59,300. The second tribe of the south is counted.

So the witnesses see the host of Simeon reckoned, the second of the three tribes under Reuben’s standard. The witnesses note the figure Gill records: fifty-nine thousand three hundred fighting men, the same number as in the census of the first chapter; the camp’s order, throughout, rests on the numbering already made. They mark that Simeon, though set under Reuben’s lead, was in fact the more numerous tribe, mustering more men than Reuben its leader; the place of leadership in the camp was not always given to the largest host, but ordered by God on other grounds, here perhaps Reuben’s right as firstborn. They observe the plain, deliberate recording that runs through the chapter: each tribe named, its captain appointed, its number told, the form unvaried, that the whole host be set in exact order. They note that the counting of Simeon, like the rest, owns the tribe and gives it its place; God numbers each, the lesser under the greater and the greater under the lesser, as he sees fit. They observe that the southern camp was gathering strength, Reuben and Simeon together already above a hundred thousand. They note that the wilderness host was a mighty and well-ordered army. They observe that every tribe stood reckoned before God. The verse thus numbers Simeon: 59,300 — Gad, the third tribe of the south, following in the next verse.

So the witnesses see in Simeon’s muster a token that God’s ordering followed not the strength of men but his own wise appointment: for Simeon, though set under Reuben’s lead, was the more numerous tribe, mustering more men than its leader. They observe that the place of command in the camp was not always given to the largest host, but ordered by God on other grounds — here, it may be, Reuben’s right as firstborn — so that honour and number did not always go together. They note the plain, deliberate recording that runs through the chapter, each tribe named, its captain appointed, its number told, that the whole be set in exact order. They mark that Simeon, whose place at his father’s blessing had been clouded, is yet owned and given its station, not cast off. They observe that God numbers each tribe, the lesser under the greater and the greater under the lesser, as he sees fit, that all may keep the place he assigns.
14:
On Numbers 2:14 — “Then the tribe of Gad: and the captain of the sons of Gad shall be Eliasaph the son of Reuel” — the witnesses mark Gad, third in the southern camp.

The Reformed Gill: Gad placed on the other side of Reuben’s standard; being the eldest son of Leah’s handmaid Zilpah, fitly placed under Leah’s sons Reuben and Simeon.

The verse adds Gad. The third tribe of the south is set under Reuben’s standard. The southern camp is completed.

So the witnesses see Gad added as the third tribe of the southern camp, completing Reuben’s company. The witnesses note the placement Gill describes: Gad was set on the other side of Reuben’s standard from Simeon, so that, as with Judah’s camp, the leader Reuben lay in the midst, with one tribe before and one behind, the banner in the centre. They mark the fitness Gill draws out in the grouping: Gad was the eldest son of Zilpah, Leah’s handmaid, and so was fitly placed under the standard of Leah’s own sons, Reuben and Simeon; the handmaid’s son joined to the mistress’s sons, as the household of Leah was kept together. They observe the captain named: Eliasaph the son of Reuel (or Deuel), prince of Gad, set over his host as the other princes over theirs. They note the wisdom of the arrangement the witnesses keep observing: the tribes were grouped not at random but by the ties of their mothers, the sons of Leah and her handmaid encamped as one company on the south, that affection and kinship might bind the camp. They observe that Gad, though sprung from a handmaid, was given full place and honour in the host, his own captain and station, none of the tribes despised. They observe that the southern camp was now complete in its three tribes, as the eastern had been. They note that God ordered the whole with care and equity. The verse thus adds Gad: third under Reuben’s standard — his number given in the next verse.

So the witnesses see in the placing of Gad the same gracious care that ordered the whole camp: the handmaid’s son joined under the standard of Leah’s own sons, the household kept together by the ties of its mother. They observe that the leader Reuben lay in the midst of his standard, Simeon before and Gad behind, the banner in the centre, as in every camp. They note that Gad, though sprung from a bondwoman, was given full place and honour in the host, his own captain and station, none of the tribes despised for the manner of its birth. They mark that God grouped the tribes not at random but by kinship, that affection might bind each company. They observe that so in the church there is neither bond nor free that is shut out, but all are given a place and an honour about the God in the midst, gathered into one body by his wise and gracious ordering.
15:
On Numbers 2:15 — “And his host, and those that were numbered of them, were forty and five thousand and six hundred and fifty” — the witnesses mark the muster of Gad.

The Reformed Gill: the host of Gad, distinct from those of Reuben and Simeon, which together formed the camp — forty-five thousand six hundred and fifty.

The verse numbers Gad. They were 45,650. The third tribe of the south is counted.

So the witnesses see the host of Gad reckoned, completing the count of the southern camp. The witnesses note the figure Gill records: forty-five thousand six hundred and fifty fighting men, the same number as in the first chapter’s census; the camp’s arrangement, as throughout, rests on the numbering already taken. They mark Gill’s observation that the host of Gad was distinct from the hosts of Reuben and Simeon, the three together making up the one camp of the south; each tribe its own army, the three one company under Reuben’s standard. They observe that Gad, though the smallest of the three southern tribes, was yet a strong host of more than forty-five thousand, and is counted with the same care as the greater; the handmaid’s tribe is reckoned no less exactly than the sons of Leah. They note the unbroken pattern of the chapter, tribe by tribe, captain and number, that the whole host be ordered to a man. They observe that the careful counting of even the lesser tribes shows God’s regard for each part of his people; none is overlooked in the muster. They observe that the southern camp was now complete and could be totalled. They note that the host of the LORD was numbered and arrayed with exactness. The verse thus numbers Gad: 45,650 — the total of Reuben’s camp gathered in the next verse.

So the witnesses see, in the muster of Gad, the completing of the southern camp’s count, the three tribes under Reuben’s standard now fully told. They observe that the host of Gad was distinct from those of Reuben and Simeon, each tribe its own army, the three together making the one camp of the south. They note that Gad, though the smallest of the three southern tribes, was yet a strong host of many thousands, and is counted with the same care as the greater; the handmaid’s tribe reckoned no less exactly than the sons of Leah. They mark the unbroken pattern of the chapter, tribe by tribe, captain and number, that the whole host be ordered to a man and none left uncounted. They observe that the careful counting of even the lesser tribes shows God’s regard for every part of his people; none is overlooked in the muster of the LORD, but each is owned, numbered, and given a place about his dwelling.
16:
On Numbers 2:16 — “All that were numbered in the camp of Reuben were an hundred thousand and fifty and one thousand and four hundred and fifty… And they shall set forth in the second rank” — the witnesses mark the total of Reuben’s camp and its place in the march.

The Reformed Gill: all numbered in the camp of Reuben — one hundred fifty-one thousand four hundred and fifty; they set forth in the second rank.

The verse totals Reuben’s camp. The three tribes numbered 151,450, and marched second. The southern camp follows Judah.

So the witnesses see the southern camp summed and given its place, second, in the order of march. The witnesses note the total Gill records: all that were numbered in the camp of Reuben — Reuben, Simeon, and Gad together — were a hundred and fifty-one thousand four hundred and fifty men; the second-largest of the four camps, after Judah’s. They mark the order of march: “they shall set forth in the second rank” — when the host moved, the camp of Reuben followed next after Judah, marching in the second place; the order of the camp was also the order of the march, each camp moving in its appointed turn. They observe (from the next verse) that the tabernacle and the Levites went between this second camp and the two that followed, so that Judah and Reuben’s camps marched before the sanctuary and the remaining two behind it; the holy things were carried in the midst of the host. They note the orderliness the witnesses keep drawing out: not only where each tribe pitched, but when each camp moved, was fixed by God, that the great multitude might march without confusion. They observe that the second place in the march, like the first, was assigned by divine appointment, not seized by the strongest; God set the ranks. They note that the host went forward as a disciplined army, rank after rank in order. They observe that the southern camp kept its station behind the leading tribe of promise. The verse thus totals Reuben’s camp and ranks it second: 151,450, after Judah — the tabernacle setting forward in the midst in the next verse.
17:
On Numbers 2:17 — “Then the tabernacle of the congregation shall set forward with the camp of the Levites in the midst of the camp: as they encamp, so shall they set forward, every man in his place by their standards” — the witnesses mark the tabernacle and the Levites in the midst.

The Reformed Gill: the tabernacle, with the camp of the Levites, set forward in the midst; Levi’s place was around the sanctuary. Wesley: “in the midst” not strictly, but largely; in the march part of the Levites went after Judah (Numbers 10:17), and part exactly in the midst.

The verse sets the tabernacle in the midst. It moved with the Levites in the centre, each tribe keeping its place. God’s dwelling is the heart of the camp.

So the witnesses see the tabernacle, borne by the Levites, set in the very midst of the host, both in camp and march. The witnesses note the central place: “the tabernacle of the congregation shall set forward… in the midst of the camp” — God’s dwelling was the centre of the whole array, the four camps pitched about it on the four sides and the Levites immediately around it; when the host moved, the tabernacle moved in the middle, two camps before and two behind. They mark Wesley’s careful qualification of “in the midst”: it is not to be taken with strict mathematical exactness, for in the actual march the Levites and the holy things were divided, part going after Judah’s camp and part exactly in the centre (Numbers 10:17); yet in the main the sanctuary was carried in the heart of the host. They observe the constancy of the order: “as they encamp, so shall they set forward, every man in his place by their standards” — the same arrangement held whether at rest or on the march; no man left his standard, the order unbroken in motion as in pitching. They note the great truth the witnesses see in this: God dwelt in the midst of his people, his sanctuary their centre, all the tribes ordered about his presence; the tabernacle in the heart of the camp preached that the LORD was among them, their glory and defence. They observe the typology: so God dwells in the midst of his church, and so all is ordered about him. They note that the holy presence was both guarded by the host and the reason of its order. The verse thus sets the tabernacle in the midst: God’s dwelling the heart of the camp — the western camp of Ephraim following in the next verses.
18:
On Numbers 2:18 — “On the west side shall be the standard of the camp of Ephraim according to their armies: and the captain of the sons of Ephraim shall be Elishama the son of Ammihud” — the witnesses mark Ephraim’s camp on the west.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: on the west, the standard of Ephraim, with the tribes of Manasseh and Benjamin. The Reformed Gill: in the rear, of Ephraim, Manasseh, and Benjamin. Wesley: Ephraim preferred before his brother, according to the prophecy (Genesis 48:19).

The verse sets Ephraim on the west. The third camp, of Ephraim, Manasseh, and Benjamin, pitches there. The western quarter is assigned.

So the witnesses see the third camp, led by Ephraim, placed on the west side of the tabernacle, in the rear. The witnesses note the position: “on the west side shall be the standard of the camp of Ephraim” — Gill and Keil observe that this camp consisted of Ephraim, Manasseh, and Benjamin (the children and grandchildren of Rachel), gathered under Ephraim’s banner on the western quarter, behind the tabernacle. They mark the captain named: Elishama the son of Ammihud, prince of Ephraim, set over the host. They observe the striking thing the witnesses draw out, Ephraim’s precedence: though Ephraim was the younger son of Joseph, he is here set before his elder brother Manasseh, given the standard while Manasseh pitched by him; and this, Wesley notes, in fulfilment of the prophecy of dying Jacob, who crossed his hands and set the younger before the elder, foretelling that Ephraim should be the greater (Genesis 48:19). They note the providence the witnesses see: God’s ordering of the camp made good the old word of blessing, the younger preferred by sovereign grace, as so often in the patriarchal history. They observe that the western camp, being of Rachel’s line through Joseph and Benjamin, kept the principle of grouping by mother that ran through the whole arrangement. They note that Ephraim’s leading honoured both the prophecy and Joseph’s house. They observe that the camp was filling out its third quarter. The verse thus sets Ephraim on the west: the third camp assigned, the younger preferred — his number given in the next verse.
19:
On Numbers 2:19 — “And his host, and those that were numbered of them, were forty thousand and five hundred” — the witnesses mark the muster of Ephraim.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: his host and those numbered, forty thousand five hundred, as for each tribe. The Reformed Gill: the host of Ephraim, numbered as before.

The verse numbers Ephraim. They were 40,500. The leading tribe of the west is counted.

So the witnesses see the host of Ephraim reckoned, the head of the western camp. The witnesses note the figure: forty thousand five hundred, the same number, Gill observes, as in the census of the first chapter; the camp’s order rests on the numbering already made. They mark that Ephraim, though set above Manasseh and given the leading of the western camp by the old prophecy, was not the largest tribe; the precedence rested not on numbers but on the word of blessing and the sovereign choice of God, who exalts the younger and the lesser as he wills. They observe Keil and Delitzsch’s note on the recurring formula, “his host, and those that were numbered of them,” kept for every tribe alike, that each be reckoned as an army in its place. They observe that Ephraim, leading the rear camp, brought an army of forty thousand to the muster; a substantial host, though smaller than the leaders of the eastern and southern camps. They note the steady, exact recording that the whole chapter keeps: name, captain, and number for each, that the host be ordered to a man. They observe that God’s preference of Ephraim was honoured in the leading place though not in the largest number, grace and not strength setting the order. They note that the western camp was beginning to be told. The verse thus numbers Ephraim: 40,500 — Manasseh, who pitched by him, following in the next verse.

So the witnesses see in Ephraim’s muster a token that God’s precedence rests not on number but on his own sovereign word. They observe that Ephraim, set above Manasseh and given the leading of the western camp by the old prophecy of Jacob, was not the largest tribe; the standard was his by the blessing pronounced upon Joseph’s younger son, not by the strength of his host. They note that grace, and not might, set the order of the camp, the younger preferred and the lesser exalted as God willed. They mark the recurring form of the record, kept for every tribe alike, that each be reckoned as an army in its place. They observe that Ephraim led the rear division with a host of many thousands, a true company of the LORD’s army. They observe that the church likewise is ordered not by human greatness but by the sovereign appointment of her Head, who exalts the lowly and sets each in his place.
20:
On Numbers 2:20 — “And by him shall be the tribe of Manasseh: and the captain of the children of Manasseh shall be Gamaliel the son of Pedahzur” — the witnesses mark Manasseh set with Ephraim.

The Reformed Gill: Manasseh, though the elder brother, was directed to pitch by Ephraim’s standard, Ephraim being preferred to him, agreeably to the prophecy.

The verse joins Manasseh to Ephraim. The elder brother pitches by the younger’s standard. The order follows the prophecy.

So the witnesses see Manasseh placed beside Ephraim in the western camp, the elder under the younger’s banner. The witnesses note the placement: “by him shall be the tribe of Manasseh” — Manasseh pitched next to Ephraim, one of the three tribes under his standard on the west. They mark the striking submission Gill draws out: though Manasseh was the elder brother, the firstborn of Joseph, he was yet given no standard of his own but directed to pitch by Ephraim’s, the younger being preferred before him; and this, the witnesses observe, was done agreeably to the prophecy of Jacob, who had set the younger above the elder (Genesis 48:19). They observe the captain named: Gamaliel the son of Pedahzur, prince of Manasseh, set over his host as the other princes over theirs. They note that Manasseh, though thus second to his younger brother, was not slighted but kept his place, captain, and honour in the host; the prophecy ordered the precedence, but did not cast off the elder. They observe the providence the witnesses see throughout: God’s arrangement of the camp quietly fulfilled the words of the patriarchs, the sons of Joseph encamped according to the blessing pronounced upon them long before. They observe that Joseph’s two sons, Ephraim and Manasseh, kept together on the west, the house of Joseph forming the heart of the western camp. They note that even the order of tents bore witness to God’s sovereign word. The verse thus joins Manasseh to Ephraim: by him under the standard — his number given in the next verse.

So the witnesses see in the placing of Manasseh under his younger brother a quiet fulfilment of God’s sovereign word. They observe that the elder, the firstborn of Joseph, was given no standard of his own but directed to pitch by Ephraim’s, the younger preferred before him, as dying Jacob had foretold when he crossed his hands upon the lads. They note that Manasseh, though thus made second to his brother, was not slighted but kept his place, his captain, and his honour in the host; the prophecy ordered the precedence, but did not cast off the elder. They mark that God’s arrangement of the camp made good the words of the patriarchs, the sons of Joseph encamped according to the blessing pronounced upon them long before. They observe that so the LORD exalts the younger and the lesser by his own free choice, and that his people are to keep without envy the place his sovereign grace assigns.
21:
On Numbers 2:21 — “And his host, and those that were numbered of them, were thirty and two thousand and two hundred” — the witnesses mark the muster of Manasseh.

The Reformed Gill: the host of Manasseh, numbered as before — thirty-two thousand two hundred.

The verse numbers Manasseh. They were 32,200. The second tribe of the west is counted.

So the witnesses see the host of Manasseh reckoned, the second of the three tribes under Ephraim’s standard. The witnesses note the figure Gill records: thirty-two thousand two hundred fighting men, the same number as in the census of the first chapter; the camp’s order, throughout, rests on the numbering already taken. They mark that Manasseh was the smallest of all the twelve tribes at this numbering, fewer in men than any other; yet it is counted and given its place with the same care as the greatest, none being too small to be owned in the host of the LORD. They observe that Manasseh, set under his younger brother Ephraim and the least in number, was doubly humbled in the order of the camp, the elder made second and the tribe the smallest; yet God gave it its captain, its standard-fellowship, and its station. They note the plain, unvaried recording of the chapter, each tribe’s number told in turn, that the whole be reckoned to a man. They observe that the careful counting of the least tribe shows that God values each part of his people, the small no less than the great. They observe that the western camp, of Joseph’s house and Benjamin, was the smallest of the four. They note that strength and number did not determine honour in God’s ordering. The verse thus numbers Manasseh: 32,200 — Benjamin, the third tribe of the west, following in the next verse.

So the witnesses see in the muster of Manasseh that God owns and numbers even the least of his people with the same care as the greatest. They observe that Manasseh was the smallest of all the twelve tribes at this numbering, fewer in men than any other, and set besides under his younger brother Ephraim — doubly humbled in the order of the camp; yet it is given its captain, its standard-fellowship, and its place, counted as exactly as the mightiest host. They note that strength and number did not determine honour in God’s ordering, but his own wise appointment. They mark the plain, unvaried recording of the chapter, each tribe’s number told in turn, that the whole be reckoned to a man. They observe that God values each part of his people, the small no less than the great, and gives the least tribe a place about his dwelling; so in the body none is so feeble as to be without honour or use.
22:
On Numbers 2:22 — “Then the tribe of Benjamin: and the captain of the sons of Benjamin shall be Abidan the son of Gideoni” — the witnesses mark Benjamin, third in the western camp.

The Reformed Gill: Benjamin pitched under Ephraim’s standard, on the other side from Manasseh, the one before and the other behind, the standard in the middle.

The verse adds Benjamin. The third tribe of the west is set under Ephraim’s standard. The western camp is completed.

So the witnesses see Benjamin added as the third tribe of the western camp, completing Ephraim’s company. The witnesses note the placement Gill describes: Benjamin was set under Ephraim’s standard, on the side opposite Manasseh, so that, as in the other camps, the leader lay in the midst, one tribe before and one behind, the banner in the centre of the three. They mark the fitness of the grouping the witnesses have traced throughout: Benjamin was the son of Rachel, as Joseph (the father of Ephraim and Manasseh) was; so the western camp gathered the house of Rachel — her son Benjamin and her grandsons Ephraim and Manasseh — under one standard, the children of one mother kept together as the camps of Leah’s sons had been. They observe the captain named: Abidan the son of Gideoni, prince of Benjamin, set over his host. They note that Benjamin, the youngest of Jacob’s sons, is given full place and honour in the host, his own captain and station, encamped with his nearest kin. They observe that the western camp was now complete in its three tribes, all of Rachel’s line, as the eastern and southern camps had been completed. They note that God’s ordering kept the families together to the end. They observe that the whole camp was nearly arrayed about the tabernacle. The verse thus adds Benjamin: third under Ephraim’s standard — his number given in the next verse.

So the witnesses see in Benjamin’s placing the completing of the western camp, the house of Rachel gathered under one standard — her son Benjamin and her grandsons Ephraim and Manasseh — the children of one mother kept together as the camps of Leah’s sons had been. They observe that the leader lay in the midst of his standard, one tribe before and one behind, the banner in the centre, as in every camp. They note that Benjamin, the youngest of Jacob’s sons, was given full place and honour in the host, his own captain and station, encamped with his nearest kin. They mark that God’s ordering kept the families together to the end, that affection and kinship might knit each company. They observe that so the church, ordered by her Head, is bound in the ties of one new birth, the members set in their places about the God in the midst, none isolated, all gathered into one.
23:
On Numbers 2:23 — “And his host, and those that were numbered of them, were thirty and five thousand and four hundred” — the witnesses mark the muster of Benjamin.

The Reformed Gill: the host of Benjamin, numbered as before — thirty-five thousand four hundred.

The verse numbers Benjamin. They were 35,400. The third tribe of the west is counted.

So the witnesses see the host of Benjamin reckoned, completing the count of the western camp. The witnesses note the figure Gill records: thirty-five thousand four hundred fighting men, the same number as in the first chapter’s census; the camp’s arrangement, as throughout, rests on the numbering already taken. They mark that Benjamin, the youngest tribe, was yet a host of more than thirty-five thousand, larger than his kinsman Manasseh, and is counted with the same exactness as the rest. They observe that the western camp, of Rachel’s line, was the smallest of the four, its three tribes together fewer than any other camp; yet each is owned and numbered, the lesser camp reckoned as carefully as the greater. They note the unbroken pattern of the chapter, tribe by tribe, captain and number, that the whole host be ordered to a man and none left uncounted. They observe that the careful enumeration, however repetitive, testifies that God knows and numbers all his people, the least camp and the youngest tribe no less than the greatest. They observe that the western camp was now complete and could be totalled. They note that the host of the LORD was being arrayed in full. They observe that every tribe stood reckoned in its place. The verse thus numbers Benjamin: 35,400 — the total of Ephraim’s camp gathered in the next verse.

So the witnesses see, in the muster of Benjamin, the completing of the western camp’s count, the smallest of the four companies now fully told. They observe that Benjamin, the youngest tribe, was yet a host of many thousands, larger than his kinsman Manasseh, and is counted with the same exactness as the rest. They note that the western camp, of Rachel’s line, was the least of the four, its three tribes together fewer than any other camp; yet each is owned and numbered, the lesser camp reckoned as carefully as the greater. They mark the unbroken pattern of the chapter, tribe by tribe, captain and number, that the whole host be ordered to a man and none left uncounted. They observe that the careful enumeration testifies that God knows and numbers all his people, the least camp and the youngest tribe no less than the greatest, and gives to every one a place and a standard about his dwelling.
24:
On Numbers 2:24 — “All that were numbered of the camp of Ephraim were an hundred thousand and eight thousand and an hundred… And they shall go forward in the third rank” — the witnesses mark the total of Ephraim’s camp and its place in the march.

The Reformed Gill: all numbered of the camp of Ephraim — one hundred eight thousand one hundred; the smallest of all the camps, near eighty thousand fewer than Judah’s; they go forward in the third rank.

The verse totals Ephraim’s camp. The three tribes numbered 108,100, and marched third. The western camp follows the tabernacle.

So the witnesses see the western camp summed and given its place, third, in the order of march. The witnesses note the total Gill records: all that were numbered of the camp of Ephraim — Ephraim, Manasseh, and Benjamin together — were a hundred and eight thousand one hundred men; the smallest of the four camps, Gill observes, nearly eighty thousand fewer than Judah’s great host. They mark the order of march: “they shall go forward in the third rank” — the camp of Ephraim, when the host moved, followed in the third place, marching after the tabernacle and its Levites, which (with the two leading camps) went before it. They observe the arrangement the witnesses have traced: two camps (Judah and Reuben) before the sanctuary, then the tabernacle in the midst, then two camps (Ephraim and, after, Dan) behind; Ephraim led the rearward division, marching first after the holy things. They note that the western camp, though the smallest in number, kept its appointed rank, neither hurried forward nor left behind; God assigned its place as truly as the greatest camp’s. They observe that the smallness of the camp did not lessen its standing in the order; the host marched in the ranks God set, by appointment and not by strength. They note that the rear divisions followed the tabernacle, guarding the holy things behind as Judah and Reuben led before. They observe that the march, like the camp, was wholly ordered. The verse thus totals Ephraim’s camp and ranks it third: 108,100, after the tabernacle — the northern camp of Dan following in the next verses.
25:
On Numbers 2:25 — “The standard of the camp of Dan shall be on the north side by their armies: and the captain of the children of Dan shall be Ahiezer the son of Ammishaddai” — the witnesses mark Dan’s camp on the north.

The Reformed Gill: the camp of Dan on the north, the left wing of the grand army, consisting of Dan, Asher, and Naphtali; Ahiezer the son of Ammishaddai its captain.

The verse sets Dan on the north. The fourth camp, of Dan, Asher, and Naphtali, pitches there. The northern quarter is assigned.

So the witnesses see the fourth and last camp, led by Dan, placed on the north side of the tabernacle. The witnesses note the position: “the standard of the camp of Dan shall be on the north side” — Gill observes that this camp consisted of Dan, Asher, and Naphtali, gathered under Dan’s banner on the northern quarter, forming (as he puts it) the left wing of the grand army, over against Reuben’s camp on the south. They mark the captain named: Ahiezer the son of Ammishaddai, prince of Dan, set over the host. They observe the dignity given to Dan, which the witnesses note: Dan was the son of Bilhah, Rachel’s handmaid, yet he is given a standard of his own and the leading of a whole camp; he was the firstborn of the sons of the handmaids, and his tribe was numerous and warlike, fit to lead. They observe the grouping by mother once more: Dan’s camp gathered the sons of the handmaids — Dan and Naphtali (sons of Bilhah) and Asher (son of Zilpah) — the children of the bondwomen encamped together, as the sons of Leah and of Rachel had been. They note that thus the whole twelve were ordered by the ties of their mothers, every tribe with its kindred. They observe that with Dan on the north the four sides of the tabernacle were now manned, the camp complete about God’s dwelling. They note that even the handmaid’s son was given honour and command. The verse thus sets Dan on the north: the fourth camp assigned — Asher pitched by him in the next verses.
26:
On Numbers 2:26 — “And his host, and those that were numbered of them, were threescore and two thousand and seven hundred” — the witnesses mark the muster of Dan.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: his host and those numbered, sixty-two thousand seven hundred, as for each tribe. The Reformed Gill: the host of Dan, numbered as before.

The verse numbers Dan. They were 62,700. The leading tribe of the north is counted.

So the witnesses see the host of Dan reckoned, the head of the northern camp and the second-largest tribe of all. The witnesses note the figure: sixty-two thousand seven hundred, the same number, Gill observes, as in the census of the first chapter; the camp’s order rests on the numbering already made. They mark that Dan, though sprung from a handmaid, was the second most numerous tribe in all Israel, exceeded only by Judah; so great a host fitly led the northern camp and (as the next verses show) brought up the rear of the whole march, where the danger was. They observe Keil and Delitzsch’s note on the recurring formula, kept for every tribe alike, that each be reckoned as an army in its place. They observe the providence the witnesses see: that the handmaid’s son was made so numerous and strong, given the leading of a camp and the guarding of the rear; God exalted the lowly and gave honour to the tribe that birth might have slighted. They note the steady, exact recording of the chapter, name, captain, and number for each, that the host be ordered to a man. They observe that Dan’s strength was matched to its station, leading the last and rearmost camp. They note that the northern camp was beginning to be told. The verse thus numbers Dan: 62,700 — Asher, who pitched by him, following in the next verse.

So the witnesses see in the muster of Dan the providence that exalted the lowly: for Dan, though sprung from a handmaid, was the second most numerous tribe in all Israel, exceeded only by Judah. They observe that so great a host fitly led the northern camp and brought up the rear of the whole march, where the danger of attack was greatest; the handmaid’s son was given the leading of a camp and the guarding of the host behind. They note the recurring form of the record, kept for every tribe alike, that each be reckoned as an army in its place. They mark that God gave honour to the tribe that birth might have slighted, making it strong and setting it in a place of trust. They observe that so the LORD chooses and exalts the lowly for his service, that the strength and honour of his people might be seen to rest on his sovereign grace and not on the accidents of birth.
27:
On Numbers 2:27 — “And those that encamp by him shall be the tribe of Asher: and the captain of the children of Asher shall be Pagiel the son of Ocran” — the witnesses mark Asher set with Dan.

The Reformed Gill: Asher pitched by Dan, who had a standard though the son of a handmaid, being the firstborn of the handmaids’ sons, his tribe warlike and numerous; Asher also a handmaid’s son.

The verse joins Asher to Dan. Asher pitches by him under the same standard. The handmaids’ sons are grouped on the north.

So the witnesses see Asher placed beside Dan in the northern camp, under the same banner. The witnesses note the placement: “those that encamp by him shall be the tribe of Asher” — Asher took his station next to Dan, one of the three tribes under his standard on the north. They mark the fitness Gill draws out: Dan had a standard of his own, though the son of a handmaid, being the firstborn of the sons of the handmaids and his tribe warlike and numerous; and Asher too was a handmaid’s son (of Zilpah, Leah’s maid), fitly joined under Dan’s standard, so that the camp gathered the children of the bondwomen together, as the other camps gathered the sons of the freeborn wives. They observe the captain named: Pagiel the son of Ocran, prince of Asher, set over his host. They note the consistent principle the witnesses have traced through the whole chapter: the tribes were grouped by the ties of their mothers, that kinship and affection might bind each camp; here the sons of the handmaids are kept together, none despised for their birth but given full place, standard, and captain. They observe that God’s ordering of the host honoured the bondwomen’s sons no less than the freeborn, all reckoned among his people. They observe that the northern camp, like the others, was knit by blood. They note that the camp was filling out its last quarter. The verse thus joins Asher to Dan: by him under the standard — his number given in the next verse.
28:
On Numbers 2:28 — “And his host, and those that were numbered of them, were forty and one thousand and five hundred” — the witnesses mark the muster of Asher.

The Reformed Gill: the host of Asher, numbered as before — forty-one thousand five hundred.

The verse numbers Asher. They were 41,500. The second tribe of the north is counted.

So the witnesses see the host of Asher reckoned, the second of the three tribes under Dan’s standard. The witnesses note the figure Gill records: forty-one thousand five hundred fighting men, the same number as in the census of the first chapter; the camp’s order, throughout, rests on the numbering already taken. They mark that Asher, a handmaid’s son, was yet a host of more than forty thousand, and is counted with the same care as the freeborn tribes; the children of the bondwomen are reckoned no less exactly than the sons of Leah and Rachel. They observe the plain, deliberate recording that runs through the chapter: each tribe named, its captain appointed, its number told, that the whole host be ordered to a man and none left uncounted. They note that the counting of Asher, like the rest, owns the tribe and gives it its place; God numbers each, the handmaid’s son as truly as the freeborn. They observe that the northern camp was gathering strength, Dan and Asher together already above a hundred thousand. They observe that the host of the LORD was a mighty and well-ordered army, every tribe arrayed. They note that each stood reckoned before God in its place. The verse thus numbers Asher: 41,500 — Naphtali, the third tribe of the north, following in the next verse.

So the witnesses see in the muster of Asher that the children of the bondwomen are reckoned in God’s host no less exactly than the sons of the freeborn. They observe that Asher, a handmaid’s son, was yet a host of many thousands, counted with the same care as the tribes of Leah and Rachel; the manner of his birth lessened neither his place nor his honour. They note the plain, deliberate recording that runs through the chapter, each tribe named, its captain appointed, its number told, that the whole host be ordered to a man and none left uncounted. They mark that the northern camp was gathering strength tribe by tribe, the sons of the handmaids making one mighty company under Dan’s standard. They observe that God numbers each of his people and gives to each a place, the bondwoman’s son as truly as the freeborn; so in the church there is neither bond nor free, but all are one, arrayed about the God in the midst.
29:
On Numbers 2:29 — “Then the tribe of Naphtali: and the captain of the children of Naphtali shall be Ahira the son of Enan” — the witnesses mark Naphtali, third in the northern camp.

The Reformed Gill: Naphtali pitched by the same standard of Dan, on the other side of it.

The verse adds Naphtali. The third tribe of the north is set under Dan’s standard. The northern camp is completed.

So the witnesses see Naphtali added as the third tribe of the northern camp, completing Dan’s company and the whole array. The witnesses note the placement Gill describes: Naphtali pitched by Dan’s standard, on the side opposite Asher, so that, as in every camp, the leader Dan lay in the midst, one tribe before and one behind, the banner in the centre of the three. They mark the fitness of the grouping the witnesses have traced throughout: Naphtali, like Dan, was a son of Bilhah, Rachel’s handmaid, full brother to Dan; so the northern camp gathered the sons of the handmaids — Dan and Naphtali of Bilhah, and Asher of Zilpah — the children of the bondwomen encamped as one company under one standard. They observe the captain named: Ahira the son of Enan, prince of Naphtali, set over his host as the last of the twelve princes. They note that with Naphtali the whole twelve tribes were now ordered, four camps of three about the tabernacle, every tribe with its captain, standard, and station, grouped by the ties of its mother. They observe that the camp was complete, the host fully arrayed about God’s dwelling on every side. They note that God’s ordering left no tribe without its place, the least and the handmaid’s sons honoured with the rest. They observe that the array was now ready to be totalled. The verse thus adds Naphtali: third under Dan’s standard — his number given in the next verse.

So the witnesses see in the placing of Naphtali the completing of the whole array: with this last of the twelve, the four camps of three were fully ordered about the tabernacle, every tribe with its captain, standard, and station, grouped by the ties of its mother. They observe that the leader Dan lay in the midst of his standard, one tribe before and one behind, the banner in the centre, as in every camp. They note that the sons of the handmaids were gathered on the north — Dan and Naphtali of Bilhah, Asher of Zilpah — the children of the bondwomen encamped as one, even as the sons of Leah and of Rachel had their camps. They mark that God’s ordering left no tribe without its place, the least and the handmaids’ sons honoured with the rest. They observe that the camp, now complete on every side, stood arrayed about God’s dwelling, a goodly and well-ordered host.
30:
On Numbers 2:30 — “And his host, and those that were numbered of them, were fifty and three thousand and four hundred” — the witnesses mark the muster of Naphtali.

The Reformed Gill: the host of Naphtali, numbered as before — fifty-three thousand four hundred.

The verse numbers Naphtali. They were 53,400. The third tribe of the north is counted.

So the witnesses see the host of Naphtali reckoned, completing the count of the northern camp and of all the twelve tribes. The witnesses note the figure Gill records: fifty-three thousand four hundred fighting men, the same number as in the first chapter’s census; the camp’s arrangement, as throughout, rests on the numbering already taken. They mark that Naphtali, the last of the tribes to be counted in the camps, was a strong host of more than fifty-three thousand, and is reckoned with the same care as the first; the twelfth tribe is owned as fully as Judah the first. They observe that with Naphtali numbered, every one of the twelve tribes had now been told in its place, the whole host reckoned tribe by tribe from Judah to Naphtali, none omitted. They note the unbroken pattern the chapter has kept, name, captain, and number for each, that the array be complete and exact. They observe that the careful counting of the last tribe, like the first, shows God’s regard for every part of his people; the whole multitude is numbered, each man in his place. They observe that the northern camp was now complete and could be totalled. They note that the host of the LORD stood fully arrayed and reckoned. They observe that the great muster was drawing to its sum. The verse thus numbers Naphtali: 53,400 — the total of Dan’s camp gathered in the next verse.

So the witnesses see, in the muster of Naphtali, the completing of the count of all the twelve tribes, the last reckoned as carefully as the first. They observe that Naphtali, the twelfth and final tribe to be told in the camps, was a strong host of many thousands, owned as fully as Judah at the head. They note that with Naphtali numbered, every one of the twelve had been reckoned in its place, the whole host told tribe by tribe from Judah to Naphtali, none omitted. They mark the unbroken pattern the chapter has kept, name, captain, and number for each, that the array be complete and exact. They observe that the careful counting of the last tribe, like the first, shows God’s regard for every part of his people; the whole multitude is numbered, each man in his place. They observe that the great muster now stood ready to be summed, the host of the LORD fully arrayed and reckoned.
31:
On Numbers 2:31 — “All they that were numbered in the camp of Dan were an hundred thousand and fifty and seven thousand and six hundred. They shall go hindmost with their standards” — the witnesses mark the total of Dan’s camp and its rearguard place.

The Reformed Gill: all numbered in the camp of Dan — one hundred fifty-seven thousand six hundred; the largest camp next to Judah. Wesley: the strongest camp after Judah, coming in the rear, that the tabernacle might be best guarded where the danger was greatest.

The verse totals Dan’s camp. The three tribes numbered 157,600, and marched hindmost. The strong rearguard closes the host.

So the witnesses see the northern camp summed and set as the rearguard of the whole march. The witnesses note the total Gill records: all that were numbered in the camp of Dan — Dan, Asher, and Naphtali together — were a hundred and fifty-seven thousand six hundred men; the largest of the four camps next to Judah’s. They mark the order of march: “they shall go hindmost with their standards” — the camp of Dan brought up the rear of the host, marching last of all the four camps. They observe the wisdom of this that Wesley draws out: Dan’s was the strongest camp after Judah, and so it was fitly set in the rear, as Judah, the strongest of all, marched in the front; the two mightiest camps were placed at the head and the foot of the column, that the host and the tabernacle in the midst might be best guarded where the danger of attack was greatest, before and behind. They note the providence and care in the arrangement: God did not set the ranks at random, but so ordered the march that the strong led and the strong closed, the weaker camps and the holy things protected between them. They observe that the rearguard, like the van, was a place of honour and trust, given to a mighty tribe. They note that the whole host was thus arrayed for safety as well as order, a marching army guarding its God in its midst. The verse thus totals Dan’s camp and sets it hindmost: 157,600, the rearguard — the grand total of all the camps following in the next verse.
32:
On Numbers 2:32 — “These are those which were numbered of the children of Israel by the house of their fathers: all those that were numbered of the camps… were six hundred thousand and three thousand and five hundred and fifty” — the witnesses mark the grand total of the host.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: all those numbered of the camps throughout their hosts — six hundred and three thousand five hundred and fifty. The Reformed Gill: the number taken by Moses and Aaron, assisted by the twelve princes, now captains over them.

The verse sums the whole host. All the camps together numbered 603,550. The grand total of Israel’s army is told.

So the witnesses see the four camps summed into one grand total of the whole host of Israel. The witnesses note the sum: “all those that were numbered of the camps… were six hundred thousand and three thousand and five hundred and fifty” — the four camps together, the twelve tribes (the Levites apart), mustered six hundred and three thousand five hundred and fifty fighting men; the same grand total as in the numbering of the first chapter. They mark Gill’s note that this number was taken by Moses and Aaron, assisted by the twelve princes of the tribes, who were now also constituted captains over the hosts they had helped to number; the leaders of the census became the leaders of the camps. They observe the greatness of the host the witnesses behold: more than six hundred thousand men of war, besides women, children, and the Levites — a mighty nation, grown from the seventy souls that went down into Egypt, now arrayed as a great army about the tabernacle. They note the faithfulness of God the number declares: he had multiplied the seed of Abraham as he promised, and brought them out a great people, ordered and counted under his hand. They observe that the whole vast multitude was reckoned to a man and set in order, none lost, all numbered; God knows the full tale of his people. They observe that the host, so numbered and arrayed, was ready to march and to fight under the LORD. They note the wonder of so great a company kept in such order. The verse thus sums the host: 603,550 in all the camps — the Levites’ exemption noted in the next verse.
33:
On Numbers 2:33 — “But the Levites were not numbered among the children of Israel; as the LORD commanded Moses” — the witnesses mark the Levites set apart from the muster.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: the Levites were not numbered among the children of Israel, as the LORD commanded (Numbers 1:49). The Reformed Gill: not numbered at this time among the Israelites, but by themselves, being a camp of their own.

The verse sets the Levites apart. They were not numbered with the tribes, by God’s command. The priestly tribe stands separate.

So the witnesses see the tribe of Levi kept distinct from the muster of the armies, as God had commanded. The witnesses note the exception: “the Levites were not numbered among the children of Israel” — Gill observes that Levi was not reckoned at this time in the census of the fighting men, but counted by itself, being a camp of its own about the tabernacle; the Levites were numbered separately and by a different rule (Numbers 1:49). They mark the ground of it: “as the LORD commanded Moses” — the separation was by express divine appointment, not human choice; God had set Levi apart from the armies of Israel for the service of the sanctuary, to bear and tend the tabernacle and to encamp immediately around it. They observe the fitness the witnesses see: the tribe given wholly to God’s service was not to be counted among the men of war, nor to march in the ranks of the camps, but to keep the holy things and to stand between the congregation and the sanctuary; its place was the centre, about God’s dwelling, not the four quarters with the tribes. They note the typology some draw: as Levi was set apart for God from among the tribes, so God claims a people peculiarly his own, a holy priesthood, distinguished from the rest for his service. They observe that the order of the camp honoured the sacred ministry by giving Levi a place and reckoning all its own. They note that obedience to God’s command governed even the numbering. The verse thus sets the Levites apart: not numbered with the tribes, by God’s word — the chapter closing, in the next verse, with Israel’s obedience.
34:
On Numbers 2:34 — “And the children of Israel did according to all that the LORD commanded Moses: so they pitched by their standards, and so they set forward, every one after their families, according to the house of their fathers” — the witnesses mark Israel’s full obedience.

The Reformed Gill: the children of Israel formed themselves into camps, so many tribes to a camp, over each a captain, and a standard erected to each, by which they pitched and marched, exactly as commanded.

The verse closes the chapter. Israel did all as the LORD commanded, pitching and marching by their standards. The ordered camp is obediently kept.

So the witnesses see the chapter close with Israel’s exact and full obedience to all that God had commanded concerning the camp. The witnesses note the obedience: “the children of Israel did according to all that the LORD commanded Moses” — Gill observes that the people formed themselves into the four camps, so many tribes to a camp, with a captain over each tribe and a standard erected to each camp, and by these they both pitched their tents and set forward on the march, exactly as God had appointed; nothing was altered or neglected. They mark the completeness of it: “so they pitched by their standards, and so they set forward, every one after their families” — both at rest and on the march, each tribe and family kept its place, the order observed in motion as in pitching; the whole vast host moved and rested as one well-disciplined army. They observe the lesson the witnesses draw: the people, so often murmuring and rebellious, here yielded a ready and full obedience to God’s ordering of the camp; when God assigns each his place, his people are to keep it without envy or dispute. They note that the orderly camp, obediently kept, was a thing of beauty and strength, of which it could later be said, “How goodly are thy tents, O Jacob” (Numbers 24:5). They observe the typology: so the church, ordered by God, is to keep its appointed places and callings, each serving in his own rank about the God in its midst. They note that obedience completed the order. The verse thus closes the chapter: Israel did all as commanded, pitching and marching by their standards — the camp arrayed in obedience about the LORD.
